system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 6: Major Scale Intervals

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 6 — Major Scale Intervals

_Pages 29-31._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Chord-scale number transposition

> the student would be wise to learn the chord-scale numbers
> given below each pattern and use them for quick, easy transposition. For example, the 1-2-3-5
> pattern of No. 44 could be applied to any new chord root, since numbers only indicate pitch relation-
> ships, not the pitches themselves.

**Significance (p. 29).** This establishes the method's core abstraction — scale-degree numbers as a transposition-ready shorthand — which underlies every pattern in the chapter.

## Major vs. minor triad bottom rule

> any of the patterns which follow, having the numbers (like 1-2-3-5) could be projected
> over any tune’s entire length, when the minor forms of these patterns have been added to the fol-
> lowing major forms, since nearly all chords contained in the tune’s progression will have either a
> major triad on the bottom (i.e., 1-2-3-5) or a minor triad (i.e., 1-2-flat 3-5).

**Significance (p. 29).** This is the book's governing harmonic rule: virtually all chords reduce to major- or minor-triad bottom structures, so mastering these two pattern families covers nearly all real-world chord changes.

## 1-2-3-5 pattern composition

> This pattern is made up of the first, second, third and fifth rones of the chord scale.

**Significance (p. 23).** Explicitly names the scale-degree content of the foundational ascending fragment, anchoring every transposition and application instruction that follows.

## 5-3-2-1 descending pattern rule

> This pattern is made up of the fifth, third, second and first tones of the chord scale.
> Note that the direction is descending from starting note in each chord.

**Significance (p. 24).** Introduces the complementary descending fragment and its directional rule, completing the two-fragment vocabulary the chapter builds all subsequent exercises upon.

## Pattern usable on major-third chords

> This pattern may be
>
> used on any chord with a major third.

**Significance (p. 25).** Provides a concise harmonic scope rule for a specific pattern, telling the student the exact chord-quality condition that governs its application.

## Minor-chord descending whole-step usage

> This pattern is used on minor chords descending in whole steps.

**Significance (p. 25).** Defines a specialized harmonic context (minor chords in whole-step descent) for a pattern, equipping the student to recognize and deploy it in real progressions.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
